[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Jyv%C3%A4skyl%C3%A4_city_council_1925.jpg/250px-Jyv%C3%A4skyl%C3%A4_city_council_1925.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jyv%C3%A4skyl%C3%A4_city_council_1925.jpg)

Meeting of [Jyväskylä](/wiki/Jyv%C3%A4skyl%C3%A4 "Jyväskylä")'s [city council](/wiki/City_council "City council") in 1925

**Local government** is a generic term for the lowest tiers of [governance](/wiki/Governance "Governance") or [public administration](/wiki/Public_administration "Public administration") within a particular [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state"). A **municipal council** or **local council** is the [legislative body](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature") of a [municipality](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") or [local government area](/wiki/Local_government_area "Local government area"). Depending on the location and classification of the municipality it may be known as a **city council**, **[town council](/wiki/Town_council "Town council")**, **town board**, **community council**, **borough council**, **[rural council](/wiki/Rural_council_(disambiguation) "Rural council (disambiguation)")**, **village council**, **board of aldermen**, or **[board of selectmen](/wiki/Select_board "Select board")**.

Local governments typically constitute a subdivision of a higher-level political or administrative unit, such as a nation or state. Local governments generally act within the powers and functions assigned to them by law or directives of a higher level of government. In [federal states](/wiki/Federation "Federation"), local government generally comprises a third or fourth level of government, whereas in [unitary states](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state"), local government usually occupies the second or third level of government.

The institutions of local government vary greatly between countries, and even where similar arrangements exist, country-specific terminology often varies. Common designated names for different types of local government entities include [counties](/wiki/County "County"), [districts](/wiki/District "District"), [cities](/wiki/City "City"), [townships](/wiki/Township "Township"), [towns](/wiki/Town "Town"), [boroughs](/wiki/Borough "Borough"), [parishes](/wiki/Parish_(administrative_division) "Parish (administrative division)"), [municipalities](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality"), [municipal corporations](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation"), [shires](/wiki/Shire "Shire"), [villages](/wiki/Village "Village"), and [local government areas](/wiki/Local_government_area "Local government area"). The same term may be used in different countries to refer to local governance institutions with vastly different institutional features, powers, or functions.

In addition to general-purpose local governments, some countries have special-purpose local governments ([special districts](/wiki/Special_district_(United_States) "Special district (United States)")), such as independent [school districts](/wiki/School_district "School district"), elected [water boards](/wiki/Water_board_(Netherlands) "Water board (Netherlands)"), or [local service districts](/wiki/Local_service_district_(disambiguation) "Local service district (disambiguation)").

Although there is no single, commonly accepted definition of the term, the most precise definition of local governments is provided by the [International Monetary Fund (IMF)](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund"), which divides the general government sector into three sub-sectors ([central](/wiki/Central_government "Central government"), [state](/wiki/State_government "State government"), and local government)[[1]](#cite_note-1) and defines local governments as "institutional units whose fiscal, legislative, and executive authority extends over the smallest geographical areas distinguished for administrative and political purposes."[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)

The IMF further notes that local governments "typically provide a wide range of services to local residents," while "the scope of their authority is generally much less than that of central government or state governments, and they may or may not be entitled to levy taxes." They are often heavily dependent on grants (transfers) from higher levels of government, and they may also act, to some extent, as agents of central or regional governments. They should also be able to appoint their own officers, independently of external administrative control. Even when local governments act as agents of central or state governments to some extent, they can be treated as a separate level of government, provided they are also able to raise and spend some funds on their own initiative and own responsibility."[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)

Other definitions for local governments are less prescriptive and emphasize the political or representative nature of local governments. For instance, OECD defines local governments as "decentralized entities whose governance bodies are elected through universal suffrage and which has general responsibilities and some autonomy with respect to budget, staff and assets."[[3]](#cite_note-3) The Local Public Sector Alliance defines local government institutions as "a corporate body (or institutional unit) that performs one or more public sector functions within a [local] jurisdiction that has adequate political, administrative, and fiscal autonomy and authority to respond to the needs and priorities of its constituents".[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Questions regarding the empowerment of local government institutions, the structure and nature of local political leadership, and the extent of local [self-governance](/wiki/Self-governance "Self-governance") and [municipal autonomy](/wiki/Municipal_autonomy "Municipal autonomy") are key questions of public administration and governance. [Local elections](/wiki/Local_election "Local election") are held in many countries.

## Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Africa")]

### Egypt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Egypt")]

Main article: [Governorates of Egypt](/wiki/Governorates_of_Egypt "Governorates of Egypt")

[Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") has a [centralised](/wiki/Centralisation "Centralisation") system of local government officially called local administration as it is a branch of the [Executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)").[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) The country is divided into 27 [governorates](/wiki/Governorates "Governorates") (محافظة **muḥāfaẓah**; Egyptian Arabic pronunciation: [[moˈħɑfzˤɑ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Egyptian_Arabic "Help:IPA/Egyptian Arabic"); [genitive case](/wiki/Genitive_case#Arabic "Genitive case"): *muḥāfaẓat* [[moˈħɑfzˤet]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Egyptian_Arabic "Help:IPA/Egyptian Arabic"); plural: محافظات **muḥāfaẓāt**[[moħɑfˈzˤɑːt]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Egyptian_Arabic "Help:IPA/Egyptian Arabic")),[[6]](#cite_note-6) the top tier of local administration. A governorate is administered by a [governor](/wiki/Governor "Governor"), who is appointed by the [President of Egypt](/wiki/President_of_Egypt "President of Egypt") and serves at the president's discretion.

Governors have the civilian rank of minister and report directly to the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Egypt "Prime Minister of Egypt"), who chairs the Board of Governors and meets with them on a regular basis.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The [Minister of Local Development](/wiki/Ministry_of_Local_Development "Ministry of Local Development") coordinates the governors and their governorate's budgets.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Mali

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mali")]

In recent years[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*], [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali") has undertaken an ambitious decentralization program, which involves the capital district of [Bamako](/wiki/Bamako "Bamako"), seven regions subdivided into 46 cercles, and 682 rural community districts (communes). The state retains an advisory role in administrative and fiscal matters, and it provides technical support, coordination, and legal recourse to these levels. Opportunities for direct political participation, and increased local responsibility for development have been improved.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In August–September 1998, elections were held for urban council members, who subsequently elected their mayors. In May/June 1999, citizens of the communes elected their communal council members for the first time. Female voter turnout was about 70% of the total, and observers considered the process open and transparent. With mayors, councils, and boards in place at the local level, newly elected officials, civil society organizations, decentralized technical services, private sector interests, other communes, and donor groups began partnering to further development.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Eventually, the cercles will be reinstituted (formerly grouping arrondissements) with a legal and financial basis of their own. Their councils will be chosen by and from members of the communal councils. The regions, at the highest decentralized level, will have a similar legal and financial autonomy, and will comprise a number of cercles within their geographical boundaries. Mali needs to build capacity at these levels, especially to mobilize and manage financial resources.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Nigeria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Nigeria")]

Main article: [Local government areas of Nigeria](/wiki/Local_government_areas_of_Nigeria "Local government areas of Nigeria")

[Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") as a [federal republic](/wiki/Federal_republic "Federal republic") operates three tiers of government: federal (or central), states
and local government. The country's [constitution](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution") provides for each local government (which exists in a single tier countrywide), and its development areas and autonomous communities created by individual state legislation to have [democratically](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") elected local government heads. There is a ministry (or bureau) of local government and chieftaincy affairs in each state charged with the responsibility of administration at that level. Nigeria has a total of 774 [Local Government Areas](/wiki/Local_Government_Areas "Local Government Areas")
(LGAs).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: South Africa")]

Main article: [Local government in South Africa](/wiki/Local_government_in_South_Africa "Local government in South Africa")

[South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") has a two-tiered local government system comprising local [municipalities](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") which fall into district municipalities, and [metropolitan municipalities](/wiki/Metropolitan_municipality_(South_Africa) "Metropolitan municipality (South Africa)") which span both tiers of local government. The country has eight metropolitan municipalities, 44 district municipalities and 205 local municipalities.

The largest cities are governed by [metropolitan municipalities](/wiki/Metropolitan_municipality_(South_Africa) "Metropolitan municipality (South Africa)"), often called "metros". An example of such is the [City of Cape Town](/wiki/City_of_Cape_Town "City of Cape Town"), which operates using a City Council (with a City Manager) as head of the executive, and a [Mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_Cape_Town "Mayor of Cape Town") as head of the ruling party locally, and overall city governance. A Deputy Mayor is also appointed.

Metropolitan municipalities govern a total of 24.8 million people, as of the [2022 Census](/wiki/2022_South_African_census "2022 South African census"), which constitutes around 40% of the South African population. Outside metros, the rest of the country is governed jointly by [district](/wiki/Districts_of_South_Africa "Districts of South Africa") and [local municipalities](/wiki/Local_municipality_(South_Africa) "Local municipality (South Africa)"). District municipalities have broader functions, such as integrated planning, infrastructure development, and public transit. Local municipalities have responsibility for all municipal functions not assigned to the district, including local service delivery. Municipalities operate within specific jurisdictions, and can set local bylaws that are quite far-reaching. However, major laws are still set by the provincial and national governments. City budgets are audited annually. Local governments (municipal councils) are elected during municipal elections, held every five years.

## Asia

### Afghanistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Afghanistan")]

Afghanistan was traditionally divided into provinces governed by centrally appointed governors with considerable autonomy in local affairs. There are currently 34 provinces. During the Soviet occupation and the development of country-wide resistance, local areas came increasingly under the control of [mujaheddin](/wiki/Mujaheddin "Mujaheddin") groups that were largely independent of any higher authority; local commanders, in some instances, asserted a measure of independence also from the mujaheddin leadership in Pakistan, establishing their own systems of local government, collecting revenues, running educational and other facilities, and even engaging in local negotiations. Mujaheddin groups retained links with the Peshawar parties to ensure access to weapons that were doled out to the parties by the government of Pakistan for distribution to fighters inside Afghanistan.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The Taliban set up a shura (assembly), made up of senior Taliban members and important tribal from the area. Each shura made laws and collected taxes locally. The Taliban set up a provisional government for the whole of Afghanistan, but it did not exercise central control over the local shuras.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The process of setting up the transitional government in June 2002 by the Loya Jirga took many steps involving local government. First, at the district and municipal level, traditional shura councils met to pick electors—persons who cast ballots for Loya Jirga delegates. Each district or municipality had to choose a predetermined number of electors, based on the size of its population. The electors then traveled to regional centers and cast ballots, to choose from amongst themselves a smaller number of loya jirga delegates— according to allotted numbers assigned to each district. The delegates then took part in the Loya Jirga.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The warlords who rule various regions of the country exert local control. The transitional government is attempting to integrate local governing authorities with the central government, but it lacks the loyalty from the warlords necessary to its governing authority. More traditional elements of political authority—such as Sufi networks, royal lineage, clan strength, age-based wisdom, and the like—still exist and play a role in Afghan society. Karzai is relying on these traditional sources of authority in his challenge to the warlords and older Islamist leaders. The deep ethnic, linguistic, sectarian, tribal, racial, and regional cleavages present in the country create what is called "Qawm" identity, emphasizing the local over higher-order formations. Qawm refers to the group to which the individual considers himself to belong, whether a subtribe, village, valley, or neighborhood. Local governing authority relies upon these forms of identity and loyalty.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Bangladesh

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Bangladesh")]

Main article: [Local government in Bangladesh](/wiki/Local_government_in_Bangladesh "Local government in Bangladesh")

Bangladesh is divided into eight administrative divisions,[[14]](#cite_note-14) each named after their respective divisional headquarters: [Barisal](/wiki/Barisal_Division "Barisal Division"), [Chittagong](/wiki/Chittagong_Division "Chittagong Division"), [Dhaka](/wiki/Dhaka_Division "Dhaka Division"), [Khulna](/wiki/Khulna_Division "Khulna Division"), [Rajshahi](/wiki/Rajshahi_Division "Rajshahi Division"), [Sylhet](/wiki/Sylhet_Division "Sylhet Division"), [Rangpur](/wiki/Rangpur_Division "Rangpur Division") and [Mymensingh Division](/wiki/Mymensingh_Division "Mymensingh Division").

Divisions are divided into *zila*. There are 64 zila in Bangladesh, each further divided into *upazila* or *thana*. The area within each police station, except for those in metropolitan areas, is divided into several *[unions](/wiki/Union_Councils_of_Bangladesh "Union Councils of Bangladesh")*, with each union consisting of multiple villages. In the metropolitan areas, police stations are divided into wards, which are further divided into *mahallas*. There are no directly elected officials at the divisional or district levels, although elected chairs of subdistricts also sit on district councils.[[15]](#cite_note-Banglapedia-15) Direct elections are held for each union (or ward), electing a chairperson and a number of members. In 1997, a parliamentary act was passed to reserve three seats (out of 12) in every union for female candidates.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Dhaka is the capital and largest city of Bangladesh. The cities with a city corporation, having mayoral elections, include Dhaka South, Dhaka North, Chittagong, Khulna, Sylhet, Rajshahi, Barisal, Rangpur, Comilla and Gazipur. Other major cities, these and other municipalities electing a mayor and councilors for each ward, include Mymensingh, Gopalganj, Jessore, Bogra, Dinajpur, Saidapur, Narayanganj, Naogaon and Rangamati. Both the municipal heads are elected for a span of five years.

### Brunei

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Brunei")]

The [administrative divisions of Brunei](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Brunei "Administrative divisions of Brunei") mainly consist of *daerah*, *mukim* and *kampung* or *kampong*. They are organised hierarchically, with *daerah* being the first level and *kampong* the third level. All the administrative divisions are under direct governance of the government through the Ministry of Home Affairs. There are four districts in Brunei: [Brunei-Muara](/wiki/Brunei-Muara_District "Brunei-Muara District"), [Belait](/wiki/Belait_District "Belait District"), [Tutong](/wiki/Tutong_District "Tutong District") and [Temburong](/wiki/Temburong_District "Temburong District"). The administrative level of mukim lies below the district. At present, there are 38 mukims, with 17 in Brunei-Muara, 8 in Tutong, 8 in Belait and 5 in Temburong District. A mukim is headed by a penghulu. A village (Malay: kampung or kampong) is the lowest administrative level in Brunei and headed by a ketua kampong or village head. Its population varies from a few hundreds to tens of thousands.

#### Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

Main articles: [Urban Council](/wiki/Urban_Council "Urban Council") and [Regional Council (Hong Kong)](/wiki/Regional_Council_(Hong_Kong) "Regional Council (Hong Kong)")

Established as the Sanitary Board in 1883, the Municipal Council in [Hong Kong Island](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Island "Hong Kong Island") and [Kowloon](/wiki/Kowloon "Kowloon") (including the [New Kowloon](/wiki/New_Kowloon "New Kowloon")) provided municipal services to the covered regions in the then [British Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong"). Partial elections were allowed in 1887, though merely enabling selected persons to vote for members of the Board. The Board was reconstituted in 1935 and hence renamed as Urban Council in the following year after the government had passed the Urban Council Ordinance. Democratisation had been implemented, allowing universal suffrage to happen throughout its development. Two years after the [Transfer of sovereignty over Hong Kong](/wiki/Transfer_of_sovereignty_over_Hong_Kong "Transfer of sovereignty over Hong Kong"), the council was disbanded in 1999 by the then [Chief Executive](/wiki/Chief_Executive_of_Hong_Kong "Chief Executive of Hong Kong") of [Hong Kong Special Administrative Region](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"). All members of the council were elected through [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") by the time of the dissolution.

The counterpart of the Municipal Council serving the [New Territories](/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories") (excluding [New Kowloon](/wiki/New_Kowloon "New Kowloon")) was the [Regional Council](/wiki/Regional_Council_(Hong_Kong) "Regional Council (Hong Kong)") established as the Provisional Regional Council in 1986. The functional select committees, district committees, and sub-committees constituted the entire Regional Council. All members were elected from the [constituencies](/wiki/Constituencies "Constituencies") and [district boards](/wiki/District_Councils_of_Hong_Kong "District Councils of Hong Kong").

Both of the Municipal Councils in Hong Kong are now defunct.

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: India")]

Main articles: [Local self-government in India](/wiki/Local_self-government_in_India "Local self-government in India") and [Municipal governance in India](/wiki/Municipal_governance_in_India "Municipal governance in India")

Further information: [Municipal council (India)](/wiki/Municipal_council_(India) "Municipal council (India)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Administrative_structure_of_India.svg/500px-Administrative_structure_of_India.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Administrative_structure_of_India.svg)

Hierarchical chart of the administrative structure of India

Local government is the third tier of government in [India](/wiki/India "India"), after [Union Government](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") and [State Government](/wiki/State_governments_of_India "State governments of India"). The urban local bodies (municipalities) are for urban governance and panchayati raj institutions (panchayats) for rural governance.

The Urban Local Bodies (ULBs):[[18]](#cite_note-18) are [Municipal Corporation](/wiki/Municipal_corporation_(India) "Municipal corporation (India)"), [Municipal Council](/wiki/Municipal_council_(India) "Municipal council (India)") (municipality) and [Town panchayat](/wiki/Nagar_panchayat "Nagar panchayat") (notified area council).

The Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs), knowns as "*Panchayats*" are a three-tier system of local self-government in rural areas in India: [District Panchayat](/wiki/District_council_(India) "District council (India)") ([district](/wiki/List_of_districts_in_India "List of districts in India") level), [Block Panchayat](/wiki/Panchayat_samiti "Panchayat samiti") ([block](/wiki/Community_development_block "Community development block") level) and [Village Panchayat](/wiki/Gram_panchayat "Gram panchayat") ([village](/wiki/Revenue_Village "Revenue Village") level).

In India, a municipal council (also referred to as a municipal committee, municipal board) functions as the elected governing body of a municipality, which is the local government institution for smaller urban areas. A municipality is generally divided into wards. Residents of each ward elect a representative called a municipal [councillor](/wiki/Councillor "Councillor"), and together these councillors constitute the municipal council.

The council is headed by a chairperson or president, assisted by a vice-chairperson or vice-president. Council meetings are presided over by the chairperson. For administrative purposes, the state government appoints a chief executive officer or municipal secretary, who oversees day-to-day operations and implements council decisions.

Municipalities are established by the respective State Municipal Acts, enacted under the framework of Part IXA of the [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India") (74th Amendment Act, 1992). The composition, powers, and functions of municipal councils are defined by state legislation.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In larger urban areas, [municipal corporations](/wiki/Municipal_corporation_(India) "Municipal corporation (India)") serve as the local governing bodies, while municipal councils generally administer smaller towns and urban centres.

### Israel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Israel")]

Main article: [Local government in Israel](/wiki/Local_government_in_Israel "Local government in Israel")

The [Israeli Ministry of Interior](/wiki/Ministry_of_Interior_(Israel) "Ministry of Interior (Israel)") recognizes four types of local government in Israel: [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [Cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Israel "List of cities in Israel"): 71 single-level urban municipalities, usually with populations exceeding 20,000 residents.
* [Local councils](/wiki/Local_council_(Israel) "Local council (Israel)"): 141 single-level urban or rural municipalities, usually with populations between 2,000 and 20,000.
* [Regional Councils](/wiki/Regional_council_(Israel) "Regional council (Israel)"): 54 bi-level municipalities which govern multiple rural communities located in relative geographic vicinity. The number of residents in the individual communities usually does not exceed 2000. There are no clear limits to the population and land area size of Israeli regional councils.
* [Industrial councils](/w/index.php?title=Local_industrial_council_(Israel)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Local industrial council (Israel) (page does not exist)"): Two single-level municipalities which govern large and complex industrial areas outside cities. The local industrial councils are Tefen in [Upper Galilee](/wiki/Upper_Galilee "Upper Galilee") (north of [Karmiel](/wiki/Karmiel "Karmiel")) and [Ramat Hovav](/wiki/Ramat_Hovav "Ramat Hovav") in the [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev") (south of [Beer Sheva](/wiki/Beer_Sheva "Beer Sheva")).

[Israeli municipal elections](/wiki/Municipal_elections_in_Israel "Municipal elections in Israel") are scheduled every five years, and apply to all local governments in the country, [Jewish](/wiki/Israeli_Jews "Israeli Jews") and [Arab](/wiki/Arab_citizens_of_Israel "Arab citizens of Israel") alike.

Since the [Meiji restoration](/wiki/Meiji_restoration "Meiji restoration"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") has had a local government system based on [prefectures](/wiki/Prefecture "Prefecture"). The national government oversees much of the country. Municipal governments were historical villages. Now mergers are common for cost effective administration. There are 47 [prefectures](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Japan "Prefectures of Japan"). They have two main responsibilities. One is mediation between national and municipal governments. The other is area wide administration. Municipal councils are parliamentary bodies established in local authorities in accordance with Article 93 of the [Constitution of Japan](/wiki/Constitution_of_Japan "Constitution of Japan") and Article 89 of the [Local Autonomy Act](/wiki/Local_Autonomy_Act "Local Autonomy Act"), etc.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The [Yokohama](/wiki/Yokohama_City_Council "Yokohama City Council") and [Osaka City Councils](/wiki/Osaka_City_Council "Osaka City Council") are examples.

### Pakistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Pakistan")]

Main article: [Local government in Pakistan](/wiki/Local_government_in_Pakistan "Local government in Pakistan")

Local government is the third tier of government in [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), after [Federal Government](/wiki/Government_of_Pakistan "Government of Pakistan") and Provincial Government. There are three types of administrative units of local government in Pakistan: [District Government Administrations](/wiki/Local_government_in_Pakistan "Local government in Pakistan"), Town Municipal Administrations and [Union Council Administrations](/wiki/Union_councils_of_Pakistan "Union councils of Pakistan")

There are over five thousand local governments in Pakistan. Since 2001, these have been led by democratically elected local councils, each headed by a Nazim (the word means "supervisor" in Urdu, but is sometimes translated as Mayor). Some districts, incorporating large metropolitan areas, are called City Districts. A City District may contain subdivisions called Towns and Union Councils. Council elections are held every four years. District Governments also include a District Coordination Officer (DCO), who is a civil servant in-charge of all devolved departments. Currently, the Powers of Nazim are also held by the DCO.

### Palestinian Authority

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Palestinian Authority")]

Main article: [Village council (Palestinian Authority)](/wiki/Village_council_(Palestinian_Authority) "Village council (Palestinian Authority)")

Local government in the [Palestinian National Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_National_Authority "Palestinian National Authority")-controlled areas are divided into three main groups: Municipal councils, village council and local development committees.

* [Municipality (Palestinian Authority)](/wiki/Municipality_(Palestinian_Authority) "Municipality (Palestinian Authority)"): Depends on size of locality. Localities that serve as the centers of [governorates](/wiki/Governorates_of_the_Palestinian_National_Authority "Governorates of the Palestinian National Authority") and populations over 15,000 have 15-member councils. Localities with populations over 15,000 residents have 13-member councils and localities with populations between 4,000 and 15,000 have 9-member councils.
* [Village Council (Palestinian Authority)](/wiki/Village_Council_(Palestinian_Authority) "Village Council (Palestinian Authority)"): Localities with populations between 800 and 1,500 have 3-member councils while those between 1,500 and −4,000 residents have 7-member councils.

### Philippines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Philippines")]

Main articles: [Local government in the Philippines](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_Philippines "Local government in the Philippines") and [Sangguniang Bayan](/wiki/Sangguniang_Bayan "Sangguniang Bayan")

See also: [Philippines § Regions and Provinces](/wiki/Philippines#Regions_and_Provinces "Philippines")

The [Local Government Code](/wiki/Local_Government_Code "Local Government Code") of 1991 provides for the three levels of Local Government Units or LGUs in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"): (1) the [province](/wiki/Philippine_province "Philippine province") (2) [city](/wiki/Philippine_city "Philippine city") and [municipality](/wiki/Philippine_municipality "Philippine municipality"), and (3) the [barangay](/wiki/Barangay "Barangay").[[22]](#cite_note-ra7160-22) The country remains a unitary state and the National Government continues to have strong influence over local government units.

A province is led by a governor along with the *[Sangguniang Panlalawigan](/wiki/Sangguniang_Panlalawigan "Sangguniang Panlalawigan")* (Provincial Council) composed of board members. A mayor leads a city or municipality while the *[Sangguniang Panlungsod](/wiki/Sangguniang_Panlungsod "Sangguniang Panlungsod")* (City Council) and the *[Sangguniang Bayan](/wiki/Sangguniang_Bayan "Sangguniang Bayan")* (Municipal Council) constitute the legislative branches of a city and municipality, respectively. A barangay is headed by the [Barangay Captain](/wiki/Barangay_Captain "Barangay Captain") and the [Barangay Council](/wiki/Barangay_Council "Barangay Council"). Barangays can be further divided into [puroks](/wiki/Purok "Purok") and [sitios](/wiki/Sitio "Sitio") but their leadership is unelected.

The 1987 Philippine Constitution also provides for the existence of autonomous regions. The [Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao](/wiki/Bangsamoro_Autonomous_Region_in_Muslim_Mindanao "Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao") (BARMM) is the only autonomous region in the Philippines. There was an attempt to institute an autonomous region in the [Cordillera](/wiki/Cordillera "Cordillera"), but that failed and instead the [Cordillera Administrative Region](/wiki/Cordillera_Administrative_Region "Cordillera Administrative Region") (CAR) was established.

Local governments have limited taxing authority. Most of their funds come from the national government via the [Internal Revenue Allotment](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Allotment "Internal Revenue Allotment")

All municipalities have their own municipal council, and these are officially called: "[Sangguniang Bayan](/wiki/Sangguniang_Bayan "Sangguniang Bayan")". [Cities](/wiki/Cities_of_the_Philippines "Cities of the Philippines") have a similar but separate form of legislature called "[Sangguniang Panlungsod](/wiki/Sangguniang_Panlungsod "Sangguniang Panlungsod")" (literally "city council"). The Local Government Code of 1991 established the current local government structure, including municipal councils.[[22]](#cite_note-ra7160-22) City councils range from 12, in most cities, to 38 members, such as the [Manila City Council](/wiki/Manila_City_Council "Manila City Council"). Members of city councils are called "councilors".

### Saudi Arabia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Saudi Arabia")]

There are three levels of local government in the Kingdom of [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia"): the city council, the municipal council and the municipality.

The city council is the highest level of local government. The municipal councils began in 2005 and is the second level of local government. The municipality is the third level of local government. There are 178 municipalities across the kingdom. The first began in Jeddah during the Othmanic period. Each municipality is run by its city's mayor. Collectively, the kingdom's municipalities make up the Ministry of Municipality and Rural Affairs (MoMRA).

### Singapore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Singapore")]

Main article: [Town Council (Singapore)](/wiki/Town_Council_(Singapore) "Town Council (Singapore)")

In Singapore, town councils are in charge of maintaining the common areas of [Housing and Development Board](/wiki/Housing_and_Development_Board "Housing and Development Board") (HDB) flats and estates, such as the common corridors, [void decks](/wiki/Void_deck "Void deck"), lifts, water tanks, external lighting and the open spaces surrounding the estates. They are regulated under the Town Councils Act ([Cap. 329A, 2000 Rev. Ed.](https://sso.agc.gov.sg/Act/TCA1988)).

The rationale was to delegate the duties of estate management to the [members of parliament](/wiki/Members_of_parliament "Members of parliament") in addition to their existing responsibilities. They would also gain management experience and be accountable to their district's resident voters.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Town councils boundaries are drawn based on [electoral districts](/wiki/Constituencies_of_Singapore "Constituencies of Singapore") boundaries. A town council area can consist of a [Group Representation Constituency](/wiki/Group_Representation_Constituency "Group Representation Constituency") (GRC), a Single Member Constituency (SMC), or a collection of neighbouring GRCs and SMCs controlled by the same political party. The Members of [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Singapore "Parliament of Singapore") head the town councils of their constituencies. Town councils boundaries do not correspond to [new town](/wiki/New_towns_of_Singapore "New towns of Singapore") boundaries; different parts of the same HDB town may be managed by different town councils.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Vietnam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Vietnam")]

[Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") has 3 levels of local government:

* First tier: municipalities (thành phố) and provinces (tỉnh)
* Second tier: provincial cities (thành phố), district-level towns (thị xã) and rural districts (huyện)
* Third tier: wards (phường), towns (thị trấn) and communes (xã)

Each level has a People's Committee (executive – up to third tier), a People's Council (legislative – up to third tier) and a People's Court (judiciary – up to second tier).

### Albania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Albania")]

Main articles: [Counties of Albania](/wiki/Counties_of_Albania "Counties of Albania"), [Districts of Albania](/wiki/Districts_of_Albania "Districts of Albania"), [Municipalities of Albania](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Albania "Municipalities of Albania"), [Administrative Units of Albania](/wiki/Administrative_Units_of_Albania "Administrative Units of Albania"), and [Villages of Albania](/wiki/Villages_of_Albania "Villages of Albania")

Since [its Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Albanian_Declaration_of_Independence "Albanian Declaration of Independence") from the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") in 1912, [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania") has reformed [its internal divisions](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Albania "Administrative divisions of Albania") 21 times. Before the implementation of the [1998 Constitution](/wiki/Albanian_Constitution "Albanian Constitution"), the primary division was into about 36 [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Albania "Districts of Albania") ([Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language"): *rrethe*), whose number, size, and importance varied over time. Following their abolishment in the year 2000, the [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Albania "Counties of Albania") were divided into [urban](/wiki/Urban_municipality_(Albania) "Urban municipality (Albania)") (*bashki*) and [rural municipalities](/wiki/Rural_municipality_(Albania) "Rural municipality (Albania)") (*komuna*)[[25]](#cite_note-25) until the [2015 elections](/wiki/2015_Albanian_local_elections "2015 Albanian local elections"), when they were replaced by the current system.

Presently, Albania has 3–4 levels of local government :

* 12 [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Albania "Counties of Albania") (*qarqet*), sometimes known as [prefectures](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Albania "Prefectures of Albania") (*prefekturë*) or administrative divisions
* 61 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Albania "Municipalities of Albania") (*bashki*)
* 373 [administrative units](/wiki/Administrative_units_of_Albania "Administrative units of Albania") ([Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language"): *njësi administrative*), sometimes known as [communes](/wiki/Communes_of_Albania "Communes of Albania") (*komuna*), which also oversee about 3,000 [villages](/wiki/Villages_of_Albania "Villages of Albania") (*fshatra*)

The prefects who oversee each county are appointed by the [central government](/wiki/Government_of_Albania "Government of Albania"), but the mayors of the municipalities and the local government councils are [elected democratically](/wiki/Elections_in_Albania "Elections in Albania").

### Andorra

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Andorra")]

[Andorra](/wiki/Andorra "Andorra") is formed by seven [parishes](/wiki/Parishes_of_Andorra "Parishes of Andorra") ([Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"): *parròquies*, singular - *parròquia*); [Andorra la Vella](/wiki/Andorra_la_Vella "Andorra la Vella"), [Canillo](/wiki/Canillo "Canillo"), [Encamp](/wiki/Encamp "Encamp"), [La Massana](/wiki/La_Massana "La Massana"), [Escaldes-Engordany](/wiki/Escaldes-Engordany "Escaldes-Engordany"), [Ordino](/wiki/Ordino "Ordino"), [Sant Julià de Lòria](/wiki/Sant_Juli%C3%A0_de_L%C3%B2ria "Sant Julià de Lòria").

Some parishes have a further territorial subdivision. Ordino, La Massana and Sant Julià de Lòria are subdivided into *quarts* (quarters), while Canillo is subdivided into 10 *veïnats* (neighborhoods). Those mostly coincide with villages, which are found in all parishes. Each parish has its own elected mayor who is the nominal head of the local government known as a *comú* in Catalan.

### Belarus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Belarus")]

Main article: [Regions of Belarus](/wiki/Regions_of_Belarus "Regions of Belarus")

At the top level of administration, [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus") is divided into six *regions* and the city of [Minsk](/wiki/Minsk "Minsk"), which has a special status being the capital of Belarus. Minsk is also the capital of [Minsk Region](/wiki/Minsk_Region "Minsk Region").

At the second level, the regions are divided into *[raions](/wiki/Raion "Raion")* ("districts").

### Bulgaria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Bulgaria")]

Main articles: [Provinces of Bulgaria](/wiki/Provinces_of_Bulgaria "Provinces of Bulgaria") and [Municipalities of Bulgaria](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Bulgaria "Municipalities of Bulgaria")

Since the 1880s, the number of territorial management units in [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria") has varied from seven to 26.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Between 1987 and 1999 the administrative structure consisted of nine provinces (*oblasti*, singular *[oblast](/wiki/Oblast "Oblast")*). A new administrative structure was adopted in parallel with the decentralisation of the economic system.[[27]](#cite_note-27) It includes 27 provinces and a metropolitan capital province (Sofia-Grad). All areas take their names from their respective capital cities. The provinces subdivide into 264 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Bulgaria "Municipalities of Bulgaria").

Municipalities are run by mayors, who are elected to four-year terms, and by directly elected municipal councils. Bulgaria is a highly [centralised](/wiki/Centralization "Centralization") state, where the national Council of Ministers directly appoints regional governors and all provinces and municipalities are heavily dependent on it for funding.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Croatia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Croatia")]

Main articles: [Counties of Croatia](/wiki/Counties_of_Croatia "Counties of Croatia"), [List of cities in Croatia](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Croatia "List of cities in Croatia"), and [Municipalities of Croatia](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Croatia "Municipalities of Croatia")

[Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") is divided into 20 counties and the capital city of [Zagreb](/wiki/Zagreb "Zagreb"), the latter having the authority and legal status of a county and a city at the same time. The counties subdivide into 127 [cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Croatia "List of cities in Croatia") and 429 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Croatia "Municipalities of Croatia").[[29]](#cite_note-CountiesAct2006-29)

### Czech Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Czech Republic")]

Main articles: [Regions of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Regions_of_the_Czech_Republic "Regions of the Czech Republic") and [Districts of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Districts_of_the_Czech_Republic "Districts of the Czech Republic")

The highest tier of local government in the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") are the [thirteen regions](/wiki/List_of_regions_of_the_Czech_Republic "List of regions of the Czech Republic") (Czech: [*kraje*, singular *kraj*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/en:kraj#Czech "wikt:en:kraj")) and the capital city of [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"). Each region has its own elected Regional Assembly (*krajské zastupitelstvo*) and *hejtman* (usually translated as [hetman](/wiki/Hetman "Hetman") or [governor](/wiki/Governor "Governor")). In Prague, their powers are executed by the city council and the mayor.

The regions are divided into seventy-six [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_the_Czech_Republic "Districts of the Czech Republic") (*okresy*, singular *[okres](/wiki/Okres "Okres")*) including three "statutory cities" (without Prague, which had special status). The districts lost most of their importance in 1999 in an administrative reform; they remain as territorial divisions and seats of various branches of state administration.[[30]](#cite_note-30) A further reform in effect since January 2003 created 204 Municipalities with Extended Competence (obce s rozšířenou působností); also obce III. stupně – third-level municipalities, unofficially also called "little districts" (Czech: 'malé okresy') which took over most of the administration of the former district authorities. Some of these are further divided between Municipalities with Commissioned Local Authority (obce s pověřeným obecním úřadem, shortened to pověřená obec, pl. pověřené obce; "second-level municipalities"). In 2007 the borders of the districts were slightly adjusted, and 119 municipalities are now within different districts.

### Denmark

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Denmark")]

Main articles: [Regions of Denmark](/wiki/Regions_of_Denmark "Regions of Denmark") and [Municipalities of Denmark](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Denmark "Municipalities of Denmark")

For local government purposes, [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") is divided into five [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Denmark "Regions of Denmark") ([Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language"): *regioner*), with their most important area of responsibility being the public health service. They are also responsible for employment policies, while some regions are responsible for public mass transit. Regions are not financial independent as they rely entirely on central state funding (around 70%) and funding coming from the municipalities (around 30%). Regions are led by directly elected councils (*regionsråd*) consisting of 41 members each.

The regions are further divided into 98 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Denmark "Municipalities of Denmark") (*kommuner*). Elections for the municipalities are held on the third Tuesday of November every four years.

Furthermore, the country is also divided into fifteen [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Estonia "Counties of Estonia") ([Estonian](/wiki/Estonian_language "Estonian language"): *maakonnad*), each of which were used to be led by a county [governor](/wiki/Governor "Governor") (*maavanem*), who represents the national government at the regional level. ### Finland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Finland")]

Main articles: [Regions of Finland](/wiki/Regions_of_Finland "Regions of Finland"), [Sub-regions of Finland](/wiki/Sub-regions_of_Finland "Sub-regions of Finland"), and [Municipalities of Finland](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Finland "Municipalities of Finland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Kaupungintalo%2C_City_Hall%2C_Vammala%2C_Sastamala_%281%29.jpg/250px-Kaupungintalo%2C_City_Hall%2C_Vammala%2C_Sastamala_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kaupungintalo,_City_Hall,_Vammala,_Sastamala_(1).jpg)

Sastamala Town Hall in [Vammala](/wiki/Vammala "Vammala"), [Sastamala](/wiki/Sastamala "Sastamala"), Finland

The most important administrative layer of local government in [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") are the 311 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Finland "Municipalities of Finland"), which may also call themselves towns or cities. They account for half of public spending. Spending is financed by municipal income tax, [property tax](/wiki/Property_tax "Property tax"), state subsidies, and other revenue.

In addition to municipalities, there are two intermediate levels of local government. Municipalities co-operate in seventy-four [sub-regions](/wiki/Sub-regions_of_Finland "Sub-regions of Finland") and nineteen [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Finland "Regions of Finland"). These are governed by the member municipalities and have only limited powers. However, the autonomous province of [Åland](/wiki/%C3%85land "Åland") has a directly elected regional council, and the [Sami people](/wiki/Sami_people "Sami people") have a semi-autonomous [Sami Domicile Area](/wiki/Sami_Domicile_Area "Sami Domicile Area") in [Lapland](/wiki/Lapland_(Finland) "Lapland (Finland)") for issues on language and culture.

### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: France")]

Main articles: [Decentralisation in France](/wiki/Decentralisation_in_France "Decentralisation in France") and [Administrative divisions of France](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_France "Administrative divisions of France")

Further information: [Municipal council (France)](/wiki/Municipal_council_(France) "Municipal council (France)") and [List of communes in France with over 20,000 inhabitants](/wiki/List_of_communes_in_France_with_over_20,000_inhabitants "List of communes in France with over 20,000 inhabitants")

According to its [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_France "Constitution of France") of 1958, [France](/wiki/France "France") has 3 levels of local government:

* 13 *Régions* (including [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica")) and 5 *Régions d'outre-mer* ([Réunion](/wiki/R%C3%A9union "Réunion"), [Martinique](/wiki/Martinique "Martinique"), [Mayotte](/wiki/Mayotte "Mayotte"), [Guadeloupe](/wiki/Guadeloupe "Guadeloupe") and [French Guiana](/wiki/French_Guiana "French Guiana")). Corsica is not referred to as a "région" but simply as a "collectivité territoriale", that merely means "local government area".
* 96 *[départements](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France")* and 5 *[départements d'outre-mer](/wiki/Overseas_department "Overseas department")* (Réunion, Guadeloupe, Martinique, [Mayotte](/wiki/Mayotte "Mayotte") and French Guiana). [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") is both a commune and a département.
* There are 36,679 [municipalities](/wiki/Communes_of_France "Communes of France") (in French: *Communes*).

However, in addition to the constitutional clauses of 1958, there now exist specificities:

* Intercommunalities are now a level of government between municipalities and departments.
* There exist 2 "pays d'outre-mer": [French Polynesia](/wiki/French_Polynesia "French Polynesia") and [New Caledonia](/wiki/New_Caledonia "New Caledonia"). The expression "pays d'outre-mer" is convenient as it can be understood in French as both "overseas country" and "overseas county/traditional area" (as evidenced by [Pays de la Loire](/wiki/Pays_de_la_Loire "Pays de la Loire") that is a home région, not a home "country"). French Polynesia works as an autonomous *région*, whereas New Caledonia has a *sui generis* local government status with specific institutions and even more autonomy.

In spite of enormous differences in populations, each of the communes of the French Republic possesses a [mayor](/wiki/Mayor_(France) "Mayor (France)") ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *maire*) and a [municipal council](/wiki/Municipal_council_(France) "Municipal council (France)") ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *conseil municipal*), which manage the [commune](/wiki/Commune_in_France "Commune in France") from the *mairie* ([city hall](/wiki/City_hall_(administration) "City hall (administration)")), with exactly the same powers no matter the size of the commune and council. This uniformity of status is a clear legacy of the French Revolution, which wanted to do away with the local idiosyncrasies and tremendous differences of status that existed in the kingdom of France. The size of a commune still matters, however, in two domains: French law determines the size of the municipal council according to the population of the commune; and the size of the population determines which voting process is used for the election of the municipal council.
