### Greece

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Greece")]

Main articles: [Modern regions of Greece](/wiki/Modern_regions_of_Greece "Modern regions of Greece") and [Municipalities and communities of Greece](/wiki/Municipalities_and_communities_of_Greece "Municipalities and communities of Greece")

Since 1 January 2011, [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") consists of thirteen [regions](/wiki/Modern_regions_of_Greece "Modern regions of Greece") subdivided into a total of 325 [municipalities and communities](/wiki/Municipalities_and_communities_of_Greece "Municipalities and communities of Greece"). The regions have their own elected governors and regional councils, however there are seven decentralized administrations, which group from one to three regions under a government-appointed general secretary. There is also one [autonomous area](/wiki/Autonomous_area "Autonomous area"), [Mount Athos](/wiki/Mount_Athos "Mount Athos").

### Hungary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Hungary")]

Main articles: [Counties of Hungary](/wiki/Counties_of_Hungary "Counties of Hungary") and [Districts of Hungary](/wiki/Districts_of_Hungary "Districts of Hungary")

For local government, [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") is divided into 19 [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Hungary "Counties of Hungary"). In addition, the [capital](/wiki/Capitals_of_Hungary "Capitals of Hungary") (*főváros*), [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest") and the 25 [Cities with county rights](/wiki/City_with_county_rights_(Hungary) "City with county rights (Hungary)") (*megyei jogú városok*) is independent of any county government. But all the county capitals are cities with county rights, except [Pest county](/wiki/Pest_county "Pest county") where Budapest is the capital of the county.

The local authorities of these cities have extended powers, but they belong to the territory of the respective county instead of being independent territorial units.

The counties are further subdivided into 198 [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Hungary "Districts of Hungary") (*járások*), and Budapest is its own [23 districts](/wiki/List_of_districts_in_Budapest "List of districts in Budapest") (*kerületek*).

### Iceland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Iceland")]

Main article: [Municipalities of Iceland](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Iceland "Municipalities of Iceland")

The [Municipalities of Iceland](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Iceland "Municipalities of Iceland") are local administrative areas in Iceland that provide a number of services to their inhabitants such as [kindergartens](/wiki/Kindergarten "Kindergarten"), [elementary schools](/wiki/Elementary_school "Elementary school"), [waste management](/wiki/Waste_management "Waste management"), [social services](/wiki/Social_services "Social services"), [public housing](/wiki/Public_housing "Public housing"), [public transportation](/wiki/Public_transportation "Public transportation"), services to [senior citizens](/wiki/Senior_citizen "Senior citizen") and [handicapped](/wiki/Disability "Disability") people. They also govern [zoning](/wiki/Zoning "Zoning") and can voluntarily take on additional functions if they have the budget for it. The autonomy of municipalities over their own matters is guaranteed by the [constitution of Iceland](/wiki/Constitution_of_Iceland "Constitution of Iceland").

The municipalities are governed by municipal councils which are directly elected every four years. The sizes of these councils vary from five members in the smallest municipalities to fifteen in the largest one. Most municipalities except for the very small ones hire an executive manager who may or may not be a member of the municipal council. These managers are usually referred to as [mayors](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") (*bæjarstjóri / borgarstjóri*) in the mostly urban municipalities but "commune manager" (*sveitarstjóri*) in the rural or mixed municipalities.

### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Ireland")]

Main article: [Local government in the Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_Republic_of_Ireland "Local government in the Republic of Ireland")

The [Republic of Ireland's](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") local government is laid out by the [Local Government Reform Act 2014](/wiki/Local_Government_Reform_Act_2014 "Local Government Reform Act 2014"). With a few exceptions, local government is two-tier. At the lowest level are the municipal, metropolitan or borough councils, which are elected during local elections. These councillors from the relevant county then together form the council, termed either County or City and County Councils. For example, the 4 municipal districts in Westmeath County each elect their own councils, who together form [Westmeath County Council](/wiki/Westmeath_County_Council "Westmeath County Council"). Many functions are performed by the Chief Executive, who is appointed by the Minister for Local Government.

The exceptions to the above is the county of Dublin and the cities of Cork and Galway, the later two's councils are directly elected with no lower council. Dublin county is made up of four local area authorities, each elected directly. There are thirty-one local authorities.

The main sources of funding for local government in Ireland are local property and motor tax revenues, payments from the Exchequer, charges for goods and services, and state grants.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Italy")]

The [Constitution of Italy](/wiki/Constitution_of_Italy "Constitution of Italy") defines three levels of local government:

* [Regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy"): they were first acknowledged after the [birth of the Italian republic](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum") in 1948. Numbering 20, they acquired a significant degree of autonomy after a constitutional reform was passed in 2001. Furthermore, 5 of them (namely Valle d'Aosta, Friuli-Venezia Giulia, Trentino-Alto Adige, Sardinia and Sicily) have a special status and are given even more power than the 15 others.
* [Provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Italy "Provinces of Italy"): they were the only local bodies in effect during the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") (from the [unification of Italy](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy") in 1861 to the birth of the Republic in 1948). Consequently, they used to serve many functions, but these were reduced as Regions absorbed more and more competences. Nowadays they number 107 and mostly care to roads, school buildings, and local zoning and planning. Finally, from 2015 onwards, 14 provinces officially became *[metropolitan cities](/wiki/Metropolitan_cities_of_Italy "Metropolitan cities of Italy")*.
* [Communes](/wiki/Comune "Comune"): The Mayor and staff, caring for the needs of a single town or of a village and neighbouring minor towns or villages.

Major cities also have an extra tier of local government named *Circoscrizione di Decentramento Comunale* or, in some cities (e.g. Rome) *Municipio*.

### Latvia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Latvia")]

Main article: [Administrative divisions of Latvia](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Latvia "Administrative divisions of Latvia")

[Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia") is a unitary state, currently divided into 110 municipalities ([Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"): *novadi*) and 9 republican cities ([Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"): *republikas pilsētas*) with their own council.

### Liechtenstein

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Liechtenstein")]

Liechtenstein is divided into eleven [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Liechtenstein "Municipalities of Liechtenstein") (*Gemeinden* – singular *Gemeinde*), most consisting of only a single town.

### Lithuania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: Lithuania")]

Main articles: [Counties of Lithuania](/wiki/Counties_of_Lithuania "Counties of Lithuania"), [Municipalities of Lithuania](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Lithuania "Municipalities of Lithuania"), and [Elderships of Lithuania](/wiki/Elderships_of_Lithuania "Elderships of Lithuania")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(June 2013)* |

Lithuania has a three-tier division of local government: the country is divided into [10 counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Lithuania "Counties of Lithuania") (Lithuanian: singular – *apskritis*, plural – *apskritys*) that are further subdivided into [60 municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Lithuania "Municipalities of Lithuania") (Lithuanian: singular – *savivaldybė*, plural – *savivaldybės*) which consist of over 500 [elderships](/wiki/Elderships_of_Lithuania "Elderships of Lithuania") (Lithuanian: singular – *seniūnija*, plural – *seniūnijos*).

The counties are ruled by [county governors](/wiki/County_governor_(Lithuania) "County governor (Lithuania)") (Lithuanian: *apskrities viršininkas*) appointed by the central government, and effectively oversee the two lower tiers of local government.

Municipalities are the most important administrative unit of local government. Each municipality has its own government and council, with elections taking place every four years. The mayor, who is a member of the council, is elected directly by the residents in a majority vote.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The council appoints [elders](/wiki/Elder_(administrative_title) "Elder (administrative title)") to govern the elderships.

Elderships, numbering over 500, are the smallest units of local government. They provide public services such as registering births and deaths and identifying individuals or families in need of welfare.

### Malta

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: Malta")]

[Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") is a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") divided into 68 municipalities ([local councils](/wiki/Local_councils_of_Malta "Local councils of Malta")), according to Chapter XA of the [Constitution of Malta](/wiki/Constitution_of_Malta "Constitution of Malta") and the Local Councils Act.

### Moldova

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: Moldova")]

The Municipal Council in [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova") is the governing body in five municipalities: [Chișinău](/wiki/Chi%C8%99in%C4%83u "Chișinău"),[[33]](#cite_note-regulament-33) [Bălți](/wiki/B%C4%83l%C8%9Bi "Bălți"), [Tiraspol](/wiki/Tiraspol "Tiraspol") and [Bendery](/wiki/Bendery "Bendery") (also known as *Tighina* or *Bender*). The Municipal Council ([Moldovan language](/wiki/Moldovan_language "Moldovan language"): *Consiliul municipal*) serves as a consultative body with some powers of general policy determination.[[33]](#cite_note-regulament-33) It is composed of a legally determined number of counsellors (for example 35 in Bălți) elected every four years, representing political parties and independent counsellors. Once elected, counsellors may form fractions inside of the Municipal Council.[[33]](#cite_note-regulament-33)

### Netherlands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=65 "Edit section: Netherlands")]

Main articles: [Municipal politics in the Netherlands](/wiki/Municipal_politics_in_the_Netherlands "Municipal politics in the Netherlands") and [Provincial politics in the Netherlands](/wiki/Provincial_politics_in_the_Netherlands "Provincial politics in the Netherlands")

The Netherlands has three tiers of government: national, provincial, municipal and water board.

#### Provincial

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=66 "Edit section: Provincial")]

The Netherlands is divided into twelve provinces (*[provincie](/wiki/Provinces_of_the_Netherlands "Provinces of the Netherlands")*, pl. *provincies*). They form the tier of administration between the central government and the municipalities. Each province is governed by a provincial council, the States-Provincial (*[Provinciale Staten](/wiki/Provinciale_Staten "Provinciale Staten")*, abbr. to *PS*). Its members are elected every four years.

The day-to-day management of the province is in the hands of the provincial executive, the States Deputed (*[Gedeputeerde Staten](/wiki/Gedeputeerde_Staten "Gedeputeerde Staten")*, abbr. to *GS*). Members of the executive are chosen by the provincial council. The size of the executive varies from one province to another. In [Flevoland](/wiki/Flevoland "Flevoland"), the smallest of the Dutch provinces, it has four members, while most other provinces have six or seven.

Meetings of the provincial executive are chaired by the King's Commissioner (*[Commissaris van de Koning(in)](/wiki/Commissaris_van_de_Koningin "Commissaris van de Koningin")*, abbr. to *CvdK*). The King's Commissioner is not elected by the residents of the province, but appointed by the Crown (the King and government ministers). The appointment is for six years and may be extended by a second term. The King's Commissioner can be dismissed only by the Crown.

King's Commissioners play an important part in the appointment of municipal mayors. When a vacancy arises, the King's Commissioner first asks the municipal council for its views as to a successor, then writes to the Minister of the Interior recommending a candidate.

#### Municipal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=67 "Edit section: Municipal")]

Municipalities (*[gemeente](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_of_the_Netherlands "List of municipalities of the Netherlands")*, pl. *gemeenten*) form the lowest tier of government in the Netherlands, after the central government and the provinces. There are 342 of them (1 January 2023)[[34]](#cite_note-gov_netherlands-34), plus three [special municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_the_Netherlands#Special_municipalities "Municipalities of the Netherlands") in the [Dutch Caribbean](/wiki/Dutch_Caribbean "Dutch Caribbean"). The municipal council (*[gemeenteraad](/wiki/Gemeenteraad "Gemeenteraad")*) is the highest authority in the municipality. Its members are elected every four years. The role of the municipal council is comparable to that of the board of an organisation or institution. Its main job is to decide the municipality's broad policies and to oversee their implementation.

The day-to-day administration of the municipality is in the hands of the municipal executive (*[college van burgemeester en wethouders](/wiki/College_van_burgemeester_en_wethouders "College van burgemeester en wethouders")*, abbr. to *(college van) B&W)*, made up of the mayor (*[burgemeester](/wiki/Burgemeester "Burgemeester")*) and the aldermen (*[wethouder](/wiki/Wethouder "Wethouder")*, pl. *wethouders*). The executive implements national legislation on matters such as social assistance, unemployment benefits and environmental management. It also bears primary responsibility for the financial affairs of the municipality and for its personnel policies.

Aldermen are appointed by the council. Councillors can be chosen to act as aldermen, resulting in their council seats being taken over by other representatives of the same political parties. Non-councillors can also be appointed.

Unlike councillors and aldermen, mayors are not elected (not even indirectly), but are appointed by the Crown. Mayors chair both the municipal council and the executive. They have a number of statutory powers and responsibilities of their own. They are responsible for maintaining public order and safety within the municipality and frequently manage the municipality's public relations. As Crown appointees, mayors also have some responsibility for overseeing the work of the municipality, its policies and relations with other government bodies. Although they are obliged to carry out the decisions of the municipal council and executive, they may recommend that the Minister of the Interior quash any decision that they believe to be contrary to the law or against the public interest.

Mayors are invariably appointed for a period of six years. They can be dismissed only by the Crown and not by the municipal council.

#### Water boards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=68 "Edit section: Water boards")]

Water boards (*[waterschap](/wiki/Waterschap "Waterschap")* and *[hoogheemraadschap](/wiki/Hoogheemraadschap "Hoogheemraadschap")*, pl. *waterschappen* and *hoogheemraadschappen*) are among the oldest government authorities in the Netherlands. They literally form the foundation of the whole Dutch system of local government; from time immemorial they have shouldered the responsibility for water management for the residents of their area. In polders this mainly involves regulating the water level. It has always been in the common interest to keep water out and polder residents have always had to work together. That is what led to the creation of water boards.

The structure of the water boards varies, but they all have a general administrative body and an executive board (*[college van dijkgraaf en heemraden](/w/index.php?title=College_van_dijkgraaf_en_heemraden&action=edit&redlink=1 "College van dijkgraaf en heemraden (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/college_van_dijkgraaf_en_heemraden "nl:college van dijkgraaf en heemraden"); [fy](https://fy.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kolleezje_fan_dykgraaf_en_hiemrieden "fy:Kolleezje fan dykgraaf en hiemrieden")]*) consisting of a chairperson (*[dijkgraaf](/wiki/Dijkgraaf_(official) "Dijkgraaf (official)")*) and other members (*[(hoog)heemraad](/wiki/Heemraad "Heemraad")*, pl. *(hoog)heemraden*). The chairperson also presides the general administrative body. This body consists of people representing the various categories of stakeholders: landholders, leaseholders, owners of buildings, companies and, since recently, all the residents as well. Importance and financial contribution decide how many representatives each category may delegate. Certain stakeholders (e.g. environmental organisations) may be given the power to appoint members.

The general administrative body elects the executive board from among its members. The government appoints the chairperson for a period of six years. The general administrative body is elected for a period of four years. They are elected as party representatives (before 2009, they were elected on an individual basis).[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Norway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=69 "Edit section: Norway")]

Norway had 357 municipalities of varying size in 2024, each administered by an elected municipal council. They are grouped into 15 counties (fylker), each governed by an elected county council.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Each county has a governor appointed by the central government, responsible for ensuring legality in their administration.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The municipal sector is a provider of vital services to the Norwegian public, accounting for about 20% of Norwegian GNP and 24% of total employment. They have the right to tax and to use their resources to support education, libraries, social security, and public works such as streetcar lines, gas and electricity works, roads, and town planning, but they are usually aided in these activities by state funds.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Oslo is the only urban center that alone constitutes a county; the remaining 14 counties consist of both urban and rural areas. County and municipal councils are popularly elected every four years.

The municipal council ([Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"): *Kommunestyre*; lit. 'municipal board') is the highest governing body of the [municipality](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_of_Norway "List of municipalities of Norway") in [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"). Municipalities are the only unit of local government in Norway (in addition to the regional "county" level and the national level). The municipal council sets the scope of municipal activity, takes major decisions, and delegates responsibility. The council is led by a [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor"), *ordfører* ([Bokmål](/wiki/Bokm%C3%A5l_language "Bokmål language")) or *ordførar* ([Nynorsk](/wiki/Nynorsk_language "Nynorsk language")), and is divided into an executive council (*formannskap*) and a number of committees, each responsible for a subsection of tasks. It is not uncommon for some members of the council to sit in the [county councils](/wiki/County_council_(Norway) "County council (Norway)") too, but very rare that they also hold national office in the [Storting](/wiki/Storting "Storting") or [Government](/wiki/Government_of_Norway "Government of Norway") without taking a [leave of absence](/wiki/Leave_of_absence "Leave of absence") from the municipal council.[[38]](#cite_note-ks-38)

The municipal council in Norway dates back the *[Formannskapsdistrikt](/wiki/Formannskapsdistrikt "Formannskapsdistrikt")* law that went into effect on 1 January 1838. Historically, the council was known as a *herredsstyre* ([Bokmål](/wiki/Bokm%C3%A5l_language "Bokmål language")) or *heradsstyre* ([Nynorsk](/wiki/Nynorsk_language "Nynorsk language")) in rural municipalities and *bystyre* in urban/city municipalities. During the 1960s, the distinction between rural and urban municipalities was eliminated, however, many Norwegian cities still refer to their municipal council as a city council (*bystyre*).[[38]](#cite_note-ks-38)

### Portugal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=70 "Edit section: Portugal")]

Main articles: [Autonomous regions of Portugal](/wiki/Autonomous_regions_of_Portugal "Autonomous regions of Portugal"), [Districts of Portugal](/wiki/Districts_of_Portugal "Districts of Portugal"), [Municipalities of Portugal](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Portugal "Municipalities of Portugal"), and [Freguesias of Portugal](/wiki/Freguesias_of_Portugal "Freguesias of Portugal")

Currently, mainland Portugal is divided into 18 districts (in Portuguese, *distritos*). Each district takes the name of their respective capital city. Insular Portugal, comprising the two [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") [archipelagos](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago") of the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores") and [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira"), is organized as two [autonomous regions](/wiki/Autonomous_regions_of_Portugal "Autonomous regions of Portugal") (in Portuguese, *regiões autónomas*).

Each district and each Autonomous region is divided into municipalities (in Portuguese, *municípios*) which, in turn, are subdivided into parishes (in Portuguese, *[freguesias](/wiki/Freguesia "Freguesia")*).

Since 1976, when the two Autonomous regions of Portugal were established, the Azores and Madeira are no longer divided into districts.

### Poland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=71 "Edit section: Poland")]

Main articles: [Administrative divisions of Poland](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Poland "Administrative divisions of Poland") and [Voivodeships of Poland](/wiki/Voivodeships_of_Poland "Voivodeships of Poland")

Poland has three levels of subdivision. The Polish territory is divided into 16 [voivodeships](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Poland "Administrative divisions of Poland") (provinces); these are further divided into 379 [powiats](/wiki/Gmina "Gmina") (counties or districts), and these powiats are further divided into 2,479 gminas (communes or municipalities). Major cities normally have the status of both gmina and powiat.

Each voivodeship is jointly governed by the National-government appointed [voivode](/wiki/Voivode "Voivode") and a locally elected [sejmik](/wiki/Voivodeship_sejmik "Voivodeship sejmik") (provincial assembly), which appoints an executive board led by a [voivodeship marshal](/wiki/Voivodeship_marshal "Voivodeship marshal").

### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=72 "Edit section: Russia")]

Main articles: [Subdivisions of Russia](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Russia "Subdivisions of Russia"), [Federal subjects of Russia](/wiki/Federal_subjects_of_Russia "Federal subjects of Russia"), and [Municipal divisions of Russia](/wiki/Municipal_divisions_of_Russia "Municipal divisions of Russia")

The Russian Federation constitutes eighty-five federal subjects that are constituent members of the Federation. All federal subjects are of equal federal rights in the sense that they have equal representation—two delegates each—in the [Federation Council](/wiki/Federation_Council_of_Russia "Federation Council of Russia") ([upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house") of the [Federal Assembly](/wiki/Federal_Assembly_of_Russia "Federal Assembly of Russia")). But they do differ in the degree of [autonomy](/wiki/Autonomous_area "Autonomous area") they enjoy. The constitution includes annexed [Russian-occupied territories](/wiki/Russian-occupied_territories "Russian-occupied territories") in dispute with Ukraine.

The modern administrative-territorial structures of the federal subjects vary significantly from one federal subject to another. While the implementation details may be considerably different, in general, however, the following types of high-level administrative divisions are recognized:

* administrative districts ([raions](/wiki/Raion "Raion"))
* [cities/towns](/wiki/City_of_federal_subject_significance "City of federal subject significance") and [urban-type settlements](/wiki/Urban-type_settlement#Administrative_divisions "Urban-type settlement") of federal subject significance
* [closed administrative-territorial formations](/wiki/Closed_city#Russia "Closed city")

Typical lower-level administrative divisions include:

* [selsoviets](/wiki/Selsoviet "Selsoviet") (rural councils)
* [towns and urban-type settlements of the administrative district significance](/wiki/Town_of_district_significance "Town of district significance")
* city districts

### Slovenia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=73 "Edit section: Slovenia")]

Slovenia has only one level of local self-government established: municipalities. The [Constitution of the Republic of Slovenia](https://www.us-rs.si/legal-basis/constitution/?lang=en) provides also basis for establishing second level, regions, but they haven't been established yet (Article 143).
There are two types of municipalities in Slovenia:

* urban municipalities (Art. 141 of the Constitution)
* (rural) municipalities (Art. 139 of the Constitution)

Slovenia signed (1994) and ratified (1996) European Charter of Local Self-Government without any reservations. The Charter is in force since March 1, 1997. The Congress of Local and Regional Authorities (Council of Europe) performed three [monitorings](https://www.coe.int/en/web/congress/congress-reports#{%2254213415%22:%5B40%5D}) (2001, 2011, and 2018).

### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=74 "Edit section: Spain")]

Main articles: [Local government in Spain](/wiki/Local_government_in_Spain "Local government in Spain"), [Municipalities of Spain](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Spain "Municipalities of Spain"), and [Comarcas of Spain](/wiki/Comarcas_of_Spain "Comarcas of Spain")

Spain is divided into 17 [autonomous communities](/wiki/Autonomous_communities_of_Spain "Autonomous communities of Spain"), which in turn are divided into 50 [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Spain "Provinces of Spain"). There are also two [autonomous cities](/wiki/Autonomous_cities_of_Spain "Autonomous cities of Spain"): those of [Ceuta](/wiki/Ceuta "Ceuta") and [Melilla](/wiki/Melilla "Melilla"). Finally, each province comprises a number of municipalities.

Each administrative entity is given powers, structure, and boundaries by a law that was passed by the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Spain "Prime Minister of Spain") .

Law 7/1985,[[39]](#cite_note-39) passed by the former Spanish Prime Minister [Felipe González Márquez](/wiki/Felipe_Gonz%C3%A1lez_M%C3%A1rquez "Felipe González Márquez") (socialist), lays down the procedure of the Local Government. Every city in Spain used this Law until 2003. This year, the former Spanish Prime Minister [José María Aznar](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Aznar "José María Aznar") (conservative), passed a Law (57/2003)[[40]](#cite_note-40) to modernize organic rules of those cities which had more than 250,000 inhabitants, and other important cities (like capital cities of provinces with at least 175,000 inhabitants). Also, it exists two other important Laws for specifically Madrid (Law 22/2006)[[41]](#cite_note-41) and Barcelona (Law 1/2006).[[42]](#cite_note-42) The main governing body in most municipalities is called Ayuntamiento (in the less populated municipalities an alternative local organization system called open council, "*concejo abierto*", is used). The Ayuntamiento in turn is formed by the Plenary (*el Pleno*, the collective formed by the city councillors) and the Mayor. The number of members that compose The Plenary varies depending on city's population (for example, since 2007 Valencia has 33 members and Pamplona has 27). The name given to the members of the Plenary is councillor (*concejal*). Those councillors are elected between city's inhabitants every four years by direct vote. After being elected, councillors meet in a special Plenary session to determine who will be elected, between them, as city's Mayor. In the next days after the election, the mayor chooses some councillors to set up the executive governing body (*Junta de Gobierno* or *Comisión de Gobierno*). After that, and for the next four years, city's mayor and the *Junta de Gobierno* will govern over the city according to their competences (urbanism, some taxes, local police, licenses for specific activities, cleaning services, etc.). Meanwhile, councillors in the Plenary but not part of the *Junta de Gobierno* (the opposition) will oversee Mayor's rule.
The autonomous community of Catalonia is divided in 4 provinces and more than 900 municipalities. Between these two tiers, there are 41 *comarques* (singular, *comarca*), roughly equivalent to 'district' or 'county'. The *comarca* is a commonwealth, or union, of municipalities with competences in several fields (Law 6/1987 of the Parliament of Catalonia).

### Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=75 "Edit section: Sweden")]

Main articles: [Municipalities of Sweden](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Sweden "Municipalities of Sweden") and [County councils of Sweden](/wiki/County_councils_of_Sweden "County councils of Sweden")

Every fourth year general elections are held in Sweden to elect members of the national parliament, 20 county council assemblies and 290 municipal assemblies. As the parliament elects the national government, the local assemblies elect their executive committees and their boards. Members in local committees and boards are elected proportionally by the political parties in the assemblies, giving all the major parties representation. The parties usually cooperate well on the local levels.

The county councils (*landsting*) are responsible for health care and usually provide transportation.

The municipalities (*kommuner*) are responsible for:

* social services, childcare, preschool, [elderly care](/wiki/Elderly_care "Elderly care")
* primary and secondary education
* planning and building
* health protection, water, sewerage, refuse, emergency services

On a voluntary basis, the municipalities provide sports, culture, housing, energy as well as commercial service.

The activities are financed by income taxes. Swedes pay around 20% of their taxable income to the municipality and around 11% to the county council. (The national government is financed by VAT and payroll taxes and fees.)

### Ukraine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=77 "Edit section: Ukraine")]

Main article: [Local government in Ukraine](/wiki/Local_government_in_Ukraine "Local government in Ukraine")

In [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), almost all cities have their City Council, and the mayors (usually elected by people) are the chairmen of them (e.g.: [Kyiv City Council](/wiki/Kyiv_City_Council "Kyiv City Council"), [Kharkiv City Council](/wiki/Kharkiv_City_Council "Kharkiv City Council"), [Odesa City Council](/wiki/Odesa_City_Council "Odesa City Council"), [Lviv City Council](/wiki/Lviv_City_Council "Lviv City Council"), [Chernihiv City Council](/wiki/Chernihiv_City_Council "Chernihiv City Council")).

### United Kingdom and dependencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=78 "Edit section: United Kingdom and dependencies")]

See also: [Administrative geography of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Administrative_geography_of_the_United_Kingdom "Administrative geography of the United Kingdom")

Main articles: [Community council](/wiki/Community_council "Community council") and [City councils in the United Kingdom](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom#City_councils "City status in the United Kingdom")

The system of local government is different in each of the four home nations of the UK. In total there are 426 local authorities in the UK. 346 of these are in England, 11 in Northern Ireland, 32 in Scotland and 22 are in Wales.

Apart from Britain and Northern Ireland, the United Kingdom has dependent territories classed as either [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories") (of which there are 14) or [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies") (of which there are 3, with Isle of Man outlined below). These are not classed as local government entities, are legally not part of the United Kingdom, and are legally self-governing territories with their own systems of local government.

In England and Wales, the [Local Government Association](/wiki/Local_Government_Association "Local Government Association") (LGA) is the national membership body for local authorities. Its core membership is made up of 317 English councils and the 22 Welsh councils through the [Welsh Local Government Association](/wiki/Welsh_Local_Government_Association "Welsh Local Government Association"). Scotland and Northern Ireland also have analogues, outlined below.

Across the UK, the [Association for Public Service Excellence](/w/index.php?title=Association_for_Public_Service_Excellence&action=edit&redlink=1 "Association for Public Service Excellence (page does not exist)") (APSE) also works to assist local authorities to improve their frontline services. APSE works with more than 250 local authorities "to advise and share information and expertise on a broad range of frontline public services".[[43]](#cite_note-43)

#### England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=79 "Edit section: England")]

Main article: [Local government in England](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England")

See also: [Counties of England](/wiki/Counties_of_England "Counties of England")

The most complex system is in England, the result of numerous reforms and reorganisation over the centuries. The top-level of sub-national administration within England until the end of March 2012 consisted of the nine [regions](/wiki/Region_(England) "Region (England)"). The regions were used by central government for various statistical purposes, and [Government Offices for the English Regions](/wiki/Government_Offices_for_the_English_Regions "Government Offices for the English Regions") and assorted other institutions including [Regional Development Agencies](/wiki/Regional_development_agency "Regional development agency"). Regional Government Offices, Regional Development Agencies and Regional Ministers were all abolished by the [Cameron ministry](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition") in 2010. Only the London region which is a sub-region compared to the other regions of England has a directly elected government. Only one regional referendum has been held to date to seek consent for the introduction of direct elections elsewhere — by [John Prescott](/wiki/John_Prescott "John Prescott") in the northeast of England — and this was initially rejected by the people of the [North East](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England") in 2004.

The layers of elected local government vary. In different areas the highest tier of elected local government may be:

* [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_England "Counties of England"), which may be
  + single-tier [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England"), or
  + divided into [districts](/wiki/District_(England) "District (England)") (also known as boroughs in some areas)
* [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_England "Districts of England"), which are separate [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England") in some areas
* [metropolitan districts](/wiki/Metropolitan_district "Metropolitan district") (also called metropolitan boroughs) in some areas which are similar to unitary authorities, but have [joint boards](/wiki/Joint-board "Joint-board") with other districts in the same [metropolitan county](/wiki/Metropolitan_county "Metropolitan county")
* [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London"), which is divided into 32 [London boroughs](/wiki/London_borough "London borough") and the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London")

In most areas there is a lower tier of government, [civil parishes](/wiki/Civil_parishes_in_England "Civil parishes in England"), with unlimited functions and powers under the [2011 Localism Act](/wiki/Localism_Act_2011 "Localism Act 2011").

Most civil parishes are in rural areas, but if the parish is a town the [parish council](/wiki/Parish_councils_of_England "Parish councils of England") may be called a [town council](/wiki/Town_council "Town council"). In a few cases the parish is a [city](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom"), and the parish council is called a [city council](/wiki/City_council "City council").

Metropolitan counties, and a few [non-metropolitan counties](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_counties "Non-metropolitan counties"), no longer have elected councils or administrative functions, and their former functions are performed by districts. Such counties remain [ceremonial counties](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England").

#### Isle of Man

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=80 "Edit section: Isle of Man")]

Main article: [Local government in the Isle of Man](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_Isle_of_Man "Local government in the Isle of Man")

Local government on the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man"), a Crown Dependency, is partly based on the ancient parishes. There are four types of local authorities: a borough corporation, town commissioners, village commissioners, and parish commissioners.

#### Northern Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=81 "Edit section: Northern Ireland")]

Main article: [Local government in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Local_government_in_Northern_Ireland "Local government in Northern Ireland")

Since 1 April 2015 Northern Ireland is divided into 11 districts. Local government in Northern Ireland does not carry out the same range of functions as those in the rest of the United Kingdom.

The Northern Ireland Local Government Association exists to represent the interests of local government in Northern Ireland and has had a specific role in representing local government to the [Northern Ireland Executive](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Executive "Northern Ireland Executive") and the [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly").[[44]](#cite_note-:0-44) Its members are the 11 [local authorities in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Local_government_in_Northern_Ireland "Local government in Northern Ireland").[[44]](#cite_note-:0-44)

#### Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=82 "Edit section: Scotland")]

Main article: [Local government in Scotland](/wiki/Local_government_in_Scotland "Local government in Scotland")

Local government in Scotland is arranged on the lines of [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority"), with the nation divided into 32 [council areas](/wiki/Council_areas_of_Scotland "Council areas of Scotland").

The [Convention of Scottish Local Authorities](/wiki/Convention_of_Scottish_Local_Authorities "Convention of Scottish Local Authorities") (COSLA) is an [umbrella organisation](/wiki/Umbrella_organization "Umbrella organization") formed in 1975 to represent the views of Scotland's thirty-two councils to central government.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

#### Wales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=83 "Edit section: Wales")]

Main article: [Local government in Wales](/wiki/Local_government_in_Wales "Local government in Wales")

Wales has a uniform system of 22 unitary authorities, variously styled as county, county borough, city or city and county local authorities. There are also [communities](/wiki/Community_(Wales) "Community (Wales)"), equivalent to parishes.

## North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=84 "Edit section: North America")]

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=85 "Edit section: Canada")]

Main article: [Local government in Canada](/wiki/Local_government_in_Canada "Local government in Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Hamilton_City_Council_%2849372778288%29.jpg/250px-Hamilton_City_Council_%2849372778288%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hamilton_City_Council_(49372778288).jpg)

A meeting of the [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario") City Council

In Canada's federal system, local government is the responsibility of [provincial and territorial governments](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada").[[46]](#cite_note-46) The most prominent form of local government is the municipality, which is a locally elected authority with responsibility over a variety of services, such as roads, parks, fire protection, policing, planning, libraries, transit, and waste management.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The exact responsibilities, powers, and governance of municipalities varies from province to province. In [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), and [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), municipalities operate on a two-tiered system, where lower-tier local municipalities administer some services, such as fire protection or parks, while upper-tier regional municipalities administer shared services, such as utilities, waste management, or policing.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) The remaining provinces and territories use a single-tier system of municipal government, where each municipality is legally independent of every other, although they may still voluntarily share services.

In addition to municipal government, some provinces maintain special purpose boards to govern police services, school districts, conservation authorities, or to provide certain municipal services to unincorporated areas that would not otherwise receive them.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The federal government regulates [First Nations band governments](/wiki/Band_government "Band government"), which deliver local services to [Indigenous reserves](/wiki/Indian_reserve "Indian reserve") in the country.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Many band governments administer more than one reserve, and may participate in tribal councils, a form of voluntary regional organization for several band governments.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

#### Ontario

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=86 "Edit section: Ontario")]

Township councils in Ontario play a similar role as [city councils](/wiki/City_council "City council") in cities for smaller or low tier municipalities. Directly elected every four years, the number of councillors vary depending on the size of their municipalities. The councillors' powers and responsibilities are governed by the *[Municipal Act](/wiki/Municipal_Act "Municipal Act")* of Ontario.

#### Manitoba

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=87 "Edit section: Manitoba")]

[Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba") town council members serve primarily as a policy and direction board for the community. They consist of five to seven members with the head of council being the mayor or [reeve](/wiki/Reeve_(Canada) "Reeve (Canada)").

### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=88 "Edit section: Mexico")]

Mexico is a Federal Republic made up by 31 [states](/wiki/States_of_Mexico "States of Mexico") and [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City"). Each state is divided in *[municipios](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Mexico "Municipalities of Mexico")*, while Mexico City is divided in sixteen *[demarcaciones territoriales](/wiki/Boroughs_of_Mexico_City "Boroughs of Mexico City")* (formerly called *delegaciones*). Twenty-nine states of Mexico were created as administrative divisions by the constitution of 1917, which grants them those powers not expressly vested in the federal government; Mexico's two remaining territories, [Baja California Sur](/wiki/Baja_California_Sur "Baja California Sur") and [Quintana Roo](/wiki/Quintana_Roo "Quintana Roo"), achieved statehood on 8 October 1974, raising the total to 31.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Each state has a constitution, a governor elected for six years, and a unicameral legislature, with representatives elected by district vote in proportion to population. An ordinary session of the legislature is held annually, and extraordinary sessions may be called by the governor or the permanent committee. Bills may be introduced by legislators, by the governor, by the state supreme court, and by municipalities (a unit comparable to a US county). In addition to the 31 states, there is also [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City"), whose [Head of Government](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Mexico_City "List of mayors of Mexico City") serves as a member of the city's cabinet and its title is compared as a governor. Many state services are supported by federal subsidies.

The principal unit of state government is the municipality. Mexico's 2,378 municipalities are governed by municipal presidents and municipal councils. State governors generally select the nominees for the municipal elections. Municipal budgets are approved by the respective state governors. Until 1997, the president appointed the mayor of Mexico City. Political reforms allowed the first open elections in 1997, and [Cuauhtémoc Cardenas Solórzano](/wiki/Cuauht%C3%A9moc_Cardenas_Sol%C3%B3rzano "Cuauhtémoc Cardenas Solórzano") became Mexico City's first elected mayor.

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=89 "Edit section: United States")]

Main article: [Local government in the United States](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_United_States "Local government in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Fullerton_City_Council.jpg/250px-Fullerton_City_Council.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fullerton_City_Council.jpg)

A city council meeting in [Fullerton, California](/wiki/Fullerton,_California "Fullerton, California")

Local government in the United States refers to governmental jurisdictions below the level of the [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state"). Most states have at least two tiers of local government: [counties](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") and [municipalities](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality"). In some states, counties are divided into [townships](/wiki/Civil_township "Civil township"). There are several different types of jurisdictions at the municipal level, including the [city](/wiki/City "City"), [town](/wiki/Town "Town"), [parish](/wiki/Parish "Parish"), [borough](/wiki/Borough_(United_States) "Borough (United States)"), [village](/wiki/Village_(United_States) "Village (United States)"), [reservations](/wiki/Indian_reservation "Indian reservation") and [boundaries](/w/index.php?title=Boundaries_(United_States)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Boundaries (United States) (page does not exist)"). The types and nature of these municipal entities varies from state to state.

City councils and town boards generally consist of several (usually somewhere between 5 and 51) elected [aldermen](/wiki/Alderman "Alderman") or [councillors](/wiki/Councillor "Councillor"). In the United States, members of city councils are typically called **council member**, **council man**, **council woman**, **councilman**, or **councilwoman**, while in Canada they are typically called **councillor**.

In some cities, the [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") is a voting member of the council who serves as [chairman](/wiki/Chairman "Chairman"); in others, the mayor is the city's independent [chief executive](/wiki/Chief_executive "Chief executive") (or [strong mayor](/wiki/Strong_mayor "Strong mayor")) with [veto](/wiki/Veto "Veto") power over city council [legislation](/wiki/Legislation "Legislation"). In larger cities the council may elect other executive positions as well, such as a council [president](/wiki/President_(government_title)#Sub-national "President (government title)") and [speaker](/wiki/Speaker_(politics) "Speaker (politics)").

The council generally functions as a [parliamentary](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") or [presidential](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system") style legislative body, proposing bills, holding votes, and passing laws to help govern the city.

The role of the mayor in the council varies depending on whether or not the city uses [council–manager government](/wiki/Council%E2%80%93manager_government "Council–manager government") or [mayor–council government](/wiki/Mayor%E2%80%93council_government "Mayor–council government"), and by the nature of the statutory authority given to it by state law, city charter, or municipal ordinance.

There may also be a [mayor pro tem](/wiki/Pro_tempore#Mayor_pro_tem "Pro tempore") councilmember. In cities where the council elects the mayor for one year at a time, the mayor pro tem is in line to become the mayor in the next year. In cities where the mayor is elected by the city's voters, the mayor pro tem serves as [acting](/wiki/Acting_(law) "Acting (law)") mayor in the absence of the mayor. This position is also known as [vice mayor](/wiki/Vice_mayor "Vice mayor").

In some cities a different name for the municipal legislature is used. In [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon"), for example, it is known as the City Commission. In [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco,_California "San Francisco, California"), it is known as the [Board of Supervisors](/wiki/San_Francisco_Board_of_Supervisors "San Francisco Board of Supervisors"); San Francisco is a [consolidated city-county](/wiki/Consolidated_city-county "Consolidated city-county") and the [California](/wiki/California "California") constitution requires each county to have a [Board of Supervisors](/wiki/Board_of_Supervisors "Board of Supervisors").

[Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") constitution allows local home rule to define any type of government via a Charter Commission including a type with no Mayor (city manager) or potentially citizen's could vote to institute a [pure democracy](/wiki/Pure_democracy "Pure democracy") with no council[[55]](#cite_note-55)

#### Indiana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=90 "Edit section: Indiana")]

[Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") town council members serve as both the executive and legislative branches for small communities incorporated as towns within the state. They consist of three or five members, depending upon the town's population.

Unlike some states, Indiana council members must declare a political party affiliation, if any, when they file to run for office. Upon election in November, they are sworn in before 1 January of the following year, where they serve a four-year term. There are no state term limits affecting how many times a candidate may run for re-election to office.

The first meeting after an election, members of the town council hold an organising meeting, where they elect a president to set future agendas and act as an official spokesman for the town or as liaison between the town and state and county government.

Indiana town councils work in conjunction with an elected [town clerk](/wiki/Town_clerk "Town clerk"), who manages the day-to-day business of the municipal government. As an elected official, the town clerk is solely executive in function and operates independently of the town council. But the council has final say on budgets which clerks depend upon to operate.

In addition to a clerk, the council can authorise the hiring of other staff to run the operations of government, including law enforcement officers, [utility](/wiki/Utility "Utility") workers, park and recreation employees and [town managers](/wiki/Town_manager "Town manager"). These employees serve at the pleasure of the council.

#### Massachusetts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=91 "Edit section: Massachusetts")]

Further information: [Government of Massachusetts](/wiki/Government_of_Massachusetts "Government of Massachusetts")

Town councils in [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") are essentially [city councils](/wiki/City_council "City council") in towns which have adopted a city form of government but prefer to retain the "town of" in their names. In several communities which have adopted such a government, the official name of the community is "The City known as the Town of..." The legislative body of a legal town in Massachusetts is a [town meeting](/wiki/Town_meeting "Town meeting"); the executive board is a [board of selectmen](/wiki/Board_of_selectmen "Board of selectmen"). In addition to having the structure of a city with a mayor and council, cities in Massachusetts can enact ordinances, while towns may adopt by-laws, which are subject to the approval of the [Attorney General](/wiki/Massachusetts_Attorney_General "Massachusetts Attorney General"). City ordinances are presumed to be legal unless challenged and set aside in court.

#### Michigan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=92 "Edit section: Michigan")]

In [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), there are [257 incorporated villages](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Michigan "List of municipalities in Michigan") that are governed by village councils, which is a form of [weak–mayor](/wiki/Mayor%E2%80%93council_government "Mayor–council government") administration. Michigan does not use "town" as a defined municipality, and villages are the lowest-level form of incorporated municipality. Villages are distinct from cities in that they share certain duties with their surrounding township and are not completely autonomous. Guidelines for village governments are defined in the General Law Village Act (Act 3) of 1895. Village councils consist of elected officials, including a village president, trustees, clerk, and treasurer.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

#### New Hampshire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=93 "Edit section: New Hampshire")]

In [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire"), the **Town Council** is an elected body which serves as the [legislative](/wiki/Legislative "Legislative") and executive body of the town. The town is governed by a [charter](/wiki/Charter "Charter"), which is allowed under the [home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule") provision of the [New Hampshire Constitution](/wiki/New_Hampshire_Constitution "New Hampshire Constitution") (Pt I, Art. 39) and Title III of the New Hampshire Revised Statutes Annotated. The charter for a Town Council must meet the following requirements of [RSA 49-D:3 I. (a) – (e)](http://www.gencourt.state.nh.us/rsa/html/III/49-D/49-D-3.htm) and all other applicable laws. The basic notion of home rule in New Hampshire is that local communities are not allowed to supersede the authority specifically granted to them by the state.

#### Official Ballot Town Council

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=94 "Edit section: Official Ballot Town Council")]

The Official Ballot Town Council is a variant form of the Town Council. In the Official Ballot form of government, the town council is vested with the limited authority to vote on all matters not voted on by official ballot. The authority and restrictions on the Official Ballot town council is the same as the Town Council, except with respect to those matters specified to be voted on by official ballot. Also, the council decides what is placed on the ballot, not the registered voters.

The charter of the Official Ballot Town Council is required by law to specify specifically:

* Which budgetary items to be included on the official ballot; and
* A finalisation process for the annual budget; and
* Process for public hearings, debate, discussion and amendment of questions to be placed on the official ballot; and
* Procedures for the transfer of funds among various departments, funds, accounts and agencies as may be necessary during the year; and
* Applicability of the official ballot procedure to special elections

The charter also must specify whether a 2⁄3 or 3⁄5 majority vote is required to approve bonds or notes, with the default being 2⁄3.

#### New Jersey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=95 "Edit section: New Jersey")]

In [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), under the [Faulkner Act](/wiki/Faulkner_Act "Faulkner Act"), municipalities have a variety of models of local government. The [legislative](/wiki/Legislative "Legislative") branch follows the subdivision's style: [city](/wiki/City_(New_Jersey) "City (New Jersey)") council, [town](/wiki/Town_(New_Jersey) "Town (New Jersey)") council, [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_Jersey) "Borough (New Jersey)") council, [township](/wiki/Township_(New_Jersey) "Township (New Jersey)") committee, or [village](/wiki/Village_(New_Jersey) "Village (New Jersey)") council, except for [Newark, New Jersey](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey") which has a [municipal council](/wiki/Municipal_Council_of_Newark "Municipal Council of Newark").

#### Ohio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=96 "Edit section: Ohio")]

In [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), the default form of municipal government organization provides limitations on the legislative body of a statutory form of government. The Ohio Revised Code does not allow the local legislative authority (council) the power to review contracts nor perform administrative duties. [No review of Contracts](https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-731.05) limiting the [checks & balances](/wiki/Separation_of_powers#Checks_and_balances "Separation of powers") in a statutory form of government. A charter form of government could provide the legislative body more administrative powers, change number of seats, or change required qualifications.
The Ohio Constitution provides for local self-government powers via a charter as defined in [Section 7 of Article XVIII](https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-constitution/section-18.7) reads as follows:

> Any municipality may frame and adopt or amend a charter for its government and may, subject to the provisions of section 3 of this article, exercise thereunder all powers of local self-government.

Sections 8 and 9 of Article XVIII provide the procedures for adoption and amendment of a municipal charter.   
The initial form of Municipal Government in Ohio is called Statuary because it is based on the default form outlined in the Ohio Revised Code statute [Title 7](https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/title-7) .   
Ohio Revised code allows for a system with no Mayor and strong council, known as [Section 705.51 | City manager plan.](https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-705.51)

The City of [Kent, Ohio](/wiki/Kent,_Ohio "Kent, Ohio") has a [charter form](https://www.kentohio.org/476/City-Charter) of Government which:

> Mayor shall be recognized as head of the City government for all ceremonial purposes, but shall have no administrative duties. (Amended 11-08-05)

#### Bicameralism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=97 "Edit section: Bicameralism")]

[Bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") city councils were common in the United States until the 20th century, when many were abolished for [cost cutting](/wiki/Cost_reduction "Cost reduction") purposes and replaced with [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") legislatures. Typically, bicameral city councils were divided into Common Councils and [Boards of Aldermen](/wiki/Alderman "Alderman"), to reflect the structure of federal and state legislatures. The city of [Everett, Massachusetts](/wiki/Everett,_Massachusetts "Everett, Massachusetts") was the last to abolish its own bicameral city council (a seven-member Board of Aldermen and an 18-member Common Council) and replace it with an 11-member City Council, doing so with a November 8, 2011 referendum which took effect in 2014.

Examples include:

* [Philadelphia City Council](/wiki/Philadelphia_City_Council "Philadelphia City Council") was bicameral from 1789 to 1919, when it included a 149-member Common Council and 41-member Select Council, making it the largest municipal legislature in the United States.
* [Worcester City Council](/wiki/Worcester,_Massachusetts "Worcester, Massachusetts") – 11-member Board of Aldermen; 30-member Common Council (1848–1948)
* [Seattle City Council](/wiki/Seattle_City_Council "Seattle City Council") – nine-member Board of Aldermen; 16-member House of Delegates (1890–1896)
* [New York City Council](/wiki/New_York_City_Council "New York City Council") – a quasi-bicameral arrangement of the [New York City Board of Estimate](/wiki/New_York_City_Board_of_Estimate "New York City Board of Estimate") and the City Council until the board's abolition in 1989
* [Board of Aldermen of the City of St. Louis](/wiki/Board_of_Aldermen_of_the_City_of_St._Louis "Board of Aldermen of the City of St. Louis") – from 1877 to 1915

## Central America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=98 "Edit section: Central America")]

### Belize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=99 "Edit section: Belize")]

Main article: [Local government in Belize](/wiki/Local_government_in_Belize "Local government in Belize")

There are currently seven [town councils in Belize](/wiki/Local_government_in_Belize "Local government in Belize"). Each town council consists of a mayor and a number of councillors, who are directly elected in municipal elections every three years. Town councils in Belize are responsible for a range of functions, including street maintenance and lighting, drainage, refuse collection, public cemeteries, infrastructure, parks and playgrounds.

## Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=100 "Edit section: Oceania")]

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=101 "Edit section: Australia")]

Main article: [Local government in Australia](/wiki/Local_government_in_Australia "Local government in Australia")

Local government is the third tier of government in Australia, after federal and state.

Because of the differences in legislation between the [states](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia"), the exact definition of a city council varies. However, it is generally only those [local government areas](/wiki/Local_government_in_Australia "Local government in Australia") which have been specifically granted city status (usually on a basis of population) that are entitled to refer to themselves as cities. The official title is "Corporation of the City of \_\_\_\_\_\_" or similar.

Some of the urban areas of Australia are governed mostly by a single entity (e.g. [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane") and other [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") cities), while others may be controlled by a multitude of much smaller city councils. Also, some significant urban areas can be under the jurisdiction of otherwise rural local governments. Periodic re-alignments of boundaries attempt to rationalize these situations and adjust the deployment of assets and resources.

### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=102 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

Main article: [Local government in New Zealand](/wiki/Local_government_in_New_Zealand "Local government in New Zealand")

New Zealand has a local government system comprising two complementary sets of local authorities—[regional councils](/wiki/Regions_of_New_Zealand "Regions of New Zealand") and [territorial authorities](/wiki/Territorial_authorities_of_New_Zealand "Territorial authorities of New Zealand"). There are 78 local authorities consisting of:

* 11 regional councils, which cover much of New Zealand's land area, and
* 67 territorial authorities (comprising 53 district councils, 12 [city councils](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_New_Zealand "List of cities in New Zealand") and 2 other councils).

Six of the territorial authorities are [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority"), which also have the powers of a regional council. They are Auckland Council, Nelson City Council, the Gisborne, Marlborough and Tasman district councils, and Chatham Islands Council.

Regional council areas are based on water catchment areas, whereas territorial authorities are based on community of interest and road access. Within a regional council area there are usually many city or district councils, although city and district councils can be in multiple regional council areas.

Local councils in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") do vary in structure, but are overseen by the government department [Local Government New Zealand](/wiki/Local_Government_New_Zealand "Local Government New Zealand"). For many decades until the local government reforms of 1989, a borough with more than 20,000 people could be proclaimed a [city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_New_Zealand "List of cities in New Zealand"). The boundaries of councils tended to follow the edge of the built-up area, so little distinction was made between the urban area and the local government area. New Zealand's local government structural arrangements were significantly reformed by the [Local Government Commission](/wiki/Local_Government_Commission_(New_Zealand) "Local Government Commission (New Zealand)") in 1989 when [approximately 700 councils and special purpose bodies](/wiki/List_of_former_territorial_authorities_in_New_Zealand "List of former territorial authorities in New Zealand") were amalgamated to create 87 new local authorities. As a result, the term "city" began to take on two meanings. The word "city" came to be used in a less formal sense to describe major urban areas independent of local body boundaries. [Gisborne](/wiki/Gisborne,_New_Zealand "Gisborne, New Zealand"), for example, adamantly described itself as the first city in the world to see the new millennium. Gisborne is administered by a district council, but its status as a city is not generally disputed. Under the current law, the minimum population for a new city is 50,000.

## South America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=103 "Edit section: South America")]

### Argentina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=104 "Edit section: Argentina")]

Argentina is a federation of 23 provinces and the federal capital of [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires"). During the 19th century there was a bitter struggle between Buenos Aires and the interior provinces, and there has long been an element of tension regarding the division of powers between the central government and provincial bodies. The federal government retains control over such matters as the regulation of commerce, customs collections, currency, civil or commercial codes, or the appointment of foreign agents. The provincial governors are elected every four years.

The constitutional "national intervention" and "state of siege" powers of the president have been invoked frequently. The first of these powers was designed to "guarantee the republican form of government in the provinces." Since the adoption of the 1853 constitution, the federal government has intervened over 200 times, mostly by presidential decree. Under this authority, provincial and municipal offices may be declared vacant, appointments annulled, and local elections supervised. Between 1966 and 1973, all local legislatures were dissolved, and provincial governors were appointed by the new president. A restoration of provincial and municipal government followed the return to constitutional government in 1973. After the March 1976 coup, the federal government again intervened to remove all provincial governors and impose direct military rule over all municipalities. Since 1983, representative local government has been in force again.

Until 1996, the President appointed the mayor of Buenos Aires, and by law, the president and Congress controlled any legislation that affected the city. Constitutional reforms that year led to an elected mayoral position, and a 60-member Legislatura (legislative council), which replaced the former city council.

### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=105 "Edit section: Brazil")]

Main article: [Municipalities of Brazil](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Brazil "Municipalities of Brazil")

Brazil is a [federation](/wiki/Federation "Federation") consisting of 27 [federative units](/wiki/Federative_units_of_Brazil "Federative units of Brazil"): 26 states and one [Federal District](/wiki/Federal_District_(Brazil) "Federal District (Brazil)"). Government exists at three levels: federal, state, and municipal. The states are subdivided into 5,570 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Brazil "Municipalities of Brazil"), while the Federal District has no municipalities (divided into [administrative regions](/wiki/Administrative_regions_of_the_Federal_District_(Brazil) "Administrative regions of the Federal District (Brazil)") instead) and has powers of both a state and a municipality..

Municipal government consists of an executive branch headed by a [mayor](/wiki/Mayors_in_Brazil "Mayors in Brazil") (*Prefeito/Prefeita*), and a legislative branch (*Câmara Municipal),* serving four-year terms*.* Municipalities are enshrined in the [constitution of 1988](/wiki/Constitution_of_Brazil "Constitution of Brazil") as entities of the federation; their responsibilities are distinct from the other two levels in theory,[[59]](#cite_note-59) but overlap in practice (e.g. education, health, transportation).[[44]](#cite_note-:0-44) With their broad powers, municipalities may create their own constitutions, termed [organic law](/wiki/Organic_law "Organic law"), and cannot be overruled by state governments.[[44]](#cite_note-:0-44)

Elections at the municipal level follow a similar, partisan system to [state and federal elections](/wiki/Elections_in_Brazil "Elections in Brazil"), but take place in different years. Municipalities may have anywhere from 9 to 55 members of the *Câmara Municipal*, depending on the population.[[60]](#cite_note-60) There is no minimum or maximum population requirement for municipalities: while the average population of a municipality in 2005 was 30,099, [Borá, São Paulo state](/wiki/Bor%C3%A1 "Borá") (the least populous) had only 823 inhabitants, while [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo") (the largest) had 10.9 million inhabitants.[[44]](#cite_note-:0-44) Municipalities within a state may choose to merge or separate with approval in a [plebiscite](/wiki/Plebiscite "Plebiscite") and enacting of a state law.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

### Paraguay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=106 "Edit section: Paraguay")]

Paraguay is divided into 17 [departments](/wiki/Departments_of_Paraguay "Departments of Paraguay"), which are subdivided into districts, which, in turn, comprise municipalities (the minimum requirement for a municipality is 3,000 persons) and rural districts (partidos). A governor, elected by popular vote, runs each department. Municipal government is exercised through a municipal board, chosen by direct election, and an executive department. In the principal cities and capitals, the executive department is headed by a mayor appointed by the minister of the interior; in other localities, the mayor is appointed by the presidents of the municipal boards. Police chiefs are appointed by the central government.

### Peru

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=107 "Edit section: Peru")]

Peru is divided into [25 regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Peru "Regions of Peru") and the [province of Lima](/wiki/Lima_Province "Lima Province"). Each region has an elected government composed of a president and council that serve four-year terms.[[62]](#cite_note-62) These governments plan regional development, execute public investment projects, promote economic activities, and manage public property.[[63]](#cite_note-63) The province of Lima is administered by a city council.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The goal of devolving power to regional and municipal governments was among others to improve popular participation. [NGOs](/wiki/NGO "NGO") played an important role in the [decentralisation](/wiki/Decentralisation "Decentralisation") process and still influence local politics.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### Uruguay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Local_government&action=edit&section=108 "Edit section: Uruguay")]

Uruguay's administrative subdivisions consisted of nineteen territories called departments and governed by intendencias, which were subordinate to the central government and responsible for local administration. They enforced national laws and administered the nation's social and educational policies and institutions within their territories. These territories had limited taxing powers, but they could borrow funds and acquire property. They also had the power to establish unpaid five-member local boards or town councils in municipalities other than the departmental capital if the population was large enough to warrant such a body.

Executive authority was vested in a governor (intendente), who administered the department, and in a thirty-one-member departmental board (junta departmental), which carried out legislative functions. These functions included approval of the departmental budget and judicial actions, such as impeachment proceedings against departmental officials, including the governor. At the municipal level, a mayor (intendente municipal) assumed executive and administrative duties, carrying out resolutions made by the local board (whose members were appointed on the basis of proportional representation of the political parties). The governor was required to comply with and enforce the constitution and the laws and to promulgate the decrees enacted by the departmental board. The governor was authorized to prepare the budget, submit it for approval to the departmental board, appoint the board's employees, and, if necessary, discipline or suspend them. The governor represented the department in its relations with the national government and other departmental governments and in the negotiation of contracts with public or private agencies.

Like the governor, the members of the departmental board and the mayor were elected for five-year terms in direct, popular elections. A governor could be reelected only once, and candidates for the post had to meet the same requirements as those for a senator, in addition to being a native of the department or a resident therein for at least three years before assuming office. Departmental board members had to be at least twenty-three years of age, native born (or a legal citizen for at least three years), and a native of the department (or a resident for at least three years).

The board sat in the capital city of each department and exercised jurisdiction throughout the entire territory of the department. It could issue decrees and resolutions that it deemed necessary either on the suggestion of the governor or on its own initiative. It could approve budgets, fix the amount of taxes, request the intervention of the Accounts Tribunal for advice concerning departmental finances or administration, and remove from office—at the request of the governor—members of nonelective local departmental boards. The board also supervised local public services; public health; and primary, secondary, preparatory, industrial, and artistic education. Although Montevideo was the smallest department in terms of area (divided into twenty-three geographic zones that generally coincided with the electoral zones), its departmental board had sixty-five members in 1990; all other departments had thirty-one-member boards and a five-member executive council appointed by the departmental board, with proportional representation from the principal political parties.

Data as of December 1990[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
